business_goals: Provide a single integrating communications network to function as the Army's tactical portion of the GIG
Link FCS with higher Army echelons and the GIG
Provide a reliable and secure network with high speed, high capacity, and high quality of service (QoS)
Support mobile communications and replace legacy systems (MSE, TRI-TAC, ISYSCON, Trojan Spirit)
Deploy a user-friendly human machine interface (HMI) for the WIN-T operator
Minimize the amount of network management the WIN-T operator must perform (automate planning)
Operable and maintainable within budget and schedule
Transparent to combat users and operators
technical_constraints: Long life cycle (fielding from 2008 through 2020, expected lifetime 20+ years)
Interoperable with Army Enterprise network, FCS, Joint networks, and Coalition network
Upgradeable (early spirals, block upgrades, COTS upgrades, security upgrades)

phase_1_discovery.initial_documentation_status: The documentation was recently consolidated following the merger of two competing contractor teams. As a result, the initial architecture documentation contained inconsistencies, was excessively complex, and diagrams often had mixed heritage, leading to confusion and potential errors.
evaluator_inquiry_questions: How will the system handle the self-organization of nodes when requirements for the algorithms are not fully defined?
What are the specific interface definitions for the Service-Oriented Architecture given the reliance on emerging standards?
How does the architecture accommodate the transition from radio firmware to high-level network operations without explicit performance models?

hardware_view_description: The system must support mobile nodes (vehicles) communicating via Line-of-Sight radios and satellite links.
The topology includes tiered nodes ranging from Maneuver Battalions to Theater Rear boundaries, requiring 'on-the-move' connectivity.
runtime_constraints: Network bandwidth is severely limited during peak operational traffic, requiring strict QoS throttling.
The system must support 'on-the-move' throughput while traveling at speeds up to 25-45 mph.

questions: How does the Enterprise Application Integration Platform handle message latency across low-bandwidth satellite links?
How is loose coupling maintained between the NetOps management software and the radio transmission subsystems?

raw_candidate_scenarios.description: A collection of vehicles collects in a 'parking lot' during operations. The networks can self-organize and nodes configure themselves within 5 minutes.
A COTS product is becoming increasingly incompatible with other COTS products being used. The COTS product is replaced.
A unit is overrun during operations. The system detects the unauthorized users and generates a security alert; an authorized person can 'zeroize' the system and shut it down remotely.
A WIN-T gateway node has been corrupted due to a network intrusion. Intrusion and corruption is detected and another node takes over duties.

architectural_approaches.description: Utilizes standardized services to facilitate interoperability between Future Combat Systems (FCS), higher Army echelons, and the Global Information Grid (GIG).

addressed_attributes.rationale: Provides a common interface for disparate systems to communicate across the tactical network.
Allows specific functional components to be updated or replaced without impacting the entire system.

architectural_approaches.description: A centralized messaging backbone used to manage communication between the NetOps management software and radio transmission subsystems.

addressed_attributes.rationale: Handles message translation and routing between different subsystems.
Manages message latency and delivery across low-bandwidth satellite and radio links.

architectural_approaches.description: Algorithms that allow mobile nodes (vehicles) to dynamically discover each other and configure the network topology without manual intervention.

addressed_attributes.rationale: Supports 'on-the-move' connectivity and rapid network formation in tactical environments.
Reduces the burden on operators by automating the planning and configuration of network nodes.

architectural_approaches.description: Traffic prioritization mechanisms that manage limited network bandwidth by ensuring critical data is delivered before routine traffic during peak operational loads.

addressed_attributes.rationale: Ensures latency requirements for critical information are met during peak traffic periods.
Guarantees the delivery of time-sensitive data over constrained links.

architectural_approaches.description: The use of Commercial-Off-The-Shelf hardware and software components integrated via standard interfaces to support long life cycles and upgradeability.

addressed_attributes.rationale: Facilitates the replacement of obsolete components with newer technology over the 20+ year expected lifetime.
Reduces development costs by leveraging existing commercial technologies and standards.

children.refinement: Seamless data exchange between FCS and GIG via SOA
children.scenario_description: The system must facilitate standardized data exchange between tactical units and the Global Information Grid using Service-Oriented Architecture interfaces.

children.refinement: Critical command data delivery over satellite links in < 500ms during peak load
children.scenario_description: During peak operational traffic, C2 data must be prioritized via QoS to ensure delivery under 500ms across satellite/radio links.

children.refinement: Network reconfiguration for 50 mobile nodes within 30 seconds of topology change
children.scenario_description: The self-organizing mesh network must dynamically update routing for 50 vehicles within 30 seconds of a change in physical topology.

children.refinement: Replacement of obsolete COTS modules with minimal integration effort (< 40 man-hours)
children.scenario_description: The architecture must support the replacement of hardware or software modules with minimal integration effort to ensure long-term upgradeability.

children.refinement: Detection and mitigation of unauthorized data injection on the message bus
children.scenario_description: A rogue node attempts to inject malicious packets into the EAI Message Bus; the system must detect and isolate the node within 10 seconds.

children.refinement: Automated failover during primary link failure in DIL environments
children.scenario_description: The primary satellite link is lost; the system must automatically failover to an organic radio link within 5 seconds to maintain critical command services.

scenarios.scenario_text: The system must facilitate standardized data exchange between tactical units and the Global Information Grid using Service-Oriented Architecture interfaces.
During peak operational traffic, C2 data must be prioritized via QoS to ensure delivery under 500ms across satellite/radio links.
The self-organizing mesh network must dynamically update routing for 50 vehicles within 30 seconds of a change in physical topology.
The architecture must support the replacement of hardware or software modules with minimal integration effort to ensure long-term upgradeability.
A rogue node attempts to inject malicious packets into the EAI Message Bus; the system must detect and isolate the node within 10 seconds.
The primary satellite link is lost; the system must automatically failover to an organic radio link within 5 seconds to maintain critical command services.

scenario_reference.text: During peak operational traffic, C2 data must be prioritized via QoS to ensure delivery under 500ms across satellite/radio links.

analysis_records.environment: Peak operational load in DIL environment
analysis_records.stimulus: High volume of concurrent tactical data traffic
analysis_records.reasoning: QoS mechanisms ensure that packets marked as high-priority (C2) bypass queues occupied by routine traffic, maintaining low latency even when bandwidth is constrained.

risks.rationale: Aggressive QoS prioritization for C2 data may lead to the complete starvation of routine situational awareness data, potentially degrading the overall common operating picture.

non_risks.rationale: Static priority queuing is a mature technology that reliably guarantees latency bounds for the highest priority class.

scenario_reference.text: The self-organizing mesh network must dynamically update routing for 50 vehicles within 30 seconds of a change in physical topology.

analysis_records.stimulus: Rapid change in node positions and connectivity
analysis_records.reasoning: Distributed routing protocols allow nodes to discover neighbors and update path tables without a central controller, facilitating rapid adaptation to movement.

risks.rationale: As the number of nodes increases beyond 50, the control plane overhead for route discovery may grow exponentially, exceeding the 30s threshold.

scenario_reference.text: A rogue node attempts to inject malicious packets into the EAI Message Bus; the system must detect and isolate the node within 10 seconds.

analysis_records.reasoning: Centralizing communication through a message bus allows for a single point of inspection for anomaly detection and enforcement of security policies.

risks.description: Processing overhead of Deep Packet Inspection (DPI)
risks.rationale: Performing security inspection on every message on the bus may introduce latency that violates the 500ms performance requirement (P1).
